title: Cineraria lyratipartita
intro: Cineraria lyratipartita, also known as Cape marigold, is a flowering plant species native to the Western Cape region of South Africa, characterized by its vibrant orange and yellow flowers. The plant is valued for its medicinal properties, with reported health benefits including anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, and antimicrobial activities, which can help alleviate symptoms of various conditions such as fever, cough, and respiratory infections. The bioactive constituents of Cineraria lyratipartita include flavonoids, saponins, and phenolic acids, which contribute to its therapeutic effects. Traditionally, the plant is used to make herbal preparations such as infusions, tinctures, and decoctions, which are used to treat various ailments, including fever, sore throats, and digestive issues.
taxonomy: Cineraria lyratipartita is classified within the family Asteraceae, commonly known as the daisy or sunflower family. This family is a large and diverse group of flowering plants, often characterized by their composite flowers. The genus Cineraria, within which Cineraria lyratipartita falls, is native to tropical and subtropical regions of the world. Cineraria lyratipartita specifically is native to the tropical regions of Africa. The species is further classified within the genus Cineraria, with its specific classification being Cineraria lyratipartita.
uses: Cineraria lyratipartita has been used in traditional medicine for its antiseptic and anti-inflammatory properties. The plant's extracts are believed to have wound-healing properties and are used to treat various skin conditions. In traditional medicine, Cineraria lyratipartita is also used to treat fever, rheumatism, and other inflammatory diseases. The plant's roots and leaves are used to prepare medicinal tea and infusions, which are believed to have a soothing effect on the body. Further research is needed to confirm the efficacy and safety of Cineraria lyratipartita's medicinal uses.

side_effects: Cineraria lyratipartita, also known as the Silver Dust plant, is not typically considered for medicinal or culinary purposes, but it's known to be toxic due to containing pyrrolizidine alkaloids. When ingested, these alkaloids can cause liver damage and other adverse health effects. If the plant is handled excessively, its sap can cause skin irritation and allergic reactions in some individuals. It is essential to exercise caution when handling Cineraria lyratipartita to avoid any potential side effects. Ingesting the plant or its parts should be strictly avoided due to the risks associated with pyrrolizidine alkaloids.
